summary: Dan Shapiro shares practical advice on building a humidor as a woodworking project after ordering a large batch of cigars. He explains why humidors are ideal small projects that allow use of scrap wood and small quantities of expensive figured woods. The post focuses on sourcing Spanish cedar for the humidor lining, warning against substitutes and offering a cost-saving tip: getting free or cheap cedar cigar boxes from local cigar shops. He notes the irony of asking your local shop for help after buying cigars online.
key_points: Humidors are ideal woodworking projects because they require small amounts of wood, allowing use of scraps and expensive figured woods without high cost
Spanish cedar is essential for humidor lining — other cedar varieties will make cigars smell like a hamster cage
Local cigar shops will often sell or give away Spanish cedar cigar boxes, providing an affordable source of lining material
The post reflects a maker's mindset of resourcefulness, recycling materials, and finding clever workarounds for expensive supplies
money_quotes: NOT any other kind of cedar, or your cigars will smell like a hamster cage.
Try your local cigar shop (you know, the one you shafted by buying all your stogies online).
Enviro-conscious recycler cheapskate humidor, and nobody will be the wiser.
Humidors are great projects because you can build them from fairly small amounts of wood.
Which, if I calculate correctly, at my current rate of smoking them, is a roughly one-decade supply.
key_insight: Resourceful makers can build quality humidors cheaply by recycling Spanish cedar from cigar boxes sourced from local shops.
